1:
"And when any will offer a meat offering unto the LORD, his offering shall be of fine flour; and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon:" The witnesses note the meat offering of fine flour, with oil poured on and frankincense laid thereon. Gill notes the word for the offerer is "a soul," that is, a man, and the meat offering, or rather meal or bread offering, was of fine flour, with oil and frankincense. The witnesses note God appointing the meat offering to be of fine flour, with oil poured on and frankincense laid on it; the appointing of the meal-offering, of flour, oil, and frankincense, a thank-offering of a sweet savour — that the offering is of the finest flour, with oil and incense: "his offering shall be of fine flour; and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon"; the witnesses note the matter, "of fine flour" — not flesh, but the finest of the wheat, finely ground, the fruit of the earth and of man's labour; the witnesses note the oil, "pour oil upon it" — the oil mingled with the flour, the type of the Holy Spirit; the witnesses note the frankincense, "put frankincense thereon" — the sweet spice burnt with it, the type of the sweet savour of grace and of acceptable devotion; the witnesses note that this was a thank- and dedication-offering, not for sin, the fruit of the ground given to God; so that the meat offering is of fine flour, oil, and frankincense; and put frankincense thereon. The witnesses note And when any will offer a meat offering unto the LORD, his offering shall be of fine flour. The witnesses note and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon.

The witnesses note the meat offering of the finest flour, with oil (the Spirit) poured on and frankincense (the sweet savour of grace) laid on — a thank- and dedication-offering, the fruit of the ground given to God; shadowing Christ in his perfect humanity (the fine flour, evenly ground, without sin), full of the Spirit, and the sweet savour of his graces, offered to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointing of the meal-offering, a thank-offering of a sweet savour. The lesson the expositors draw is that the meat offering of fine flour, oil, and frankincense shadows Christ in his perfect humanity, full of the Spirit, the sweet savour of his graces. The witnesses note the fine flour: "of fine flour" — Gill notes the finest of the wheat; the fine flour; the the finest of the wheat; the finely ground; the not flesh; the the fruit of the earth. The expositors observe the the oil: "pour oil upon it" — the Spirit; the the oil; the the Spirit; the mingled; the the flour; the anointed. The witnesses press the frankincense: "put frankincense thereon" — the sweet savour; the frankincense; the the sweet savour; the of grace; the the spice; the burnt. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ's humanity: Christ's perfect humanity; the Christ's humanity; the Christ; the perfect; the humanity; the without sin. The expositors note the thank-offering: a thank- and dedication-offering, not for sin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and he shall pour oil upon it and put frankincense thereon, and the meal-offering. The verse thus records the meat offering — teaching that it was of fine flour, oil, and frankincense, shadowing Christ in his perfect humanity, full of the Spirit.
2:
"And he shall bring it to Aaron's sons the priests: and he shall take thereout his handful of the flour thereof, and of the oil thereof, with all the frankincense thereof; and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar, to be an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD:" The witnesses note the offering brought to the priests, a handful with all the frankincense burnt as a memorial, a sweet savour. Gill notes the offerer brought it to the priest and left it with him, who took out a handful of the flour and oil, with all the frankincense, and burnt it on the altar for a memorial. The witnesses note the offerer bringing the meat offering to the priests, who took a handful of the flour and oil with all the frankincense and burnt it for a memorial, a sweet savour to the LORD; the burning of a handful with all the incense, the part for the whole, a sweet savour — that a memorial-handful is burnt to God: "he shall take thereout his handful of the flour... and of the oil... with all the frankincense... and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the bringing, "bring it to Aaron's sons the priests" — that the offerer left it with the priest, who offered it; the witnesses note the handful, "his handful of the flour... and of the oil" — a token portion, the part standing for the whole; the witnesses note that "all the frankincense" was burnt, the whole of the sweet incense given to God; the witnesses note the burning, "burn the memorial of it upon the altar" — a "memorial," a part offered to put God in remembrance, the rest given to the priests (v.3); the witnesses note the acceptance, "of a sweet savour unto the LORD" — that the offering was well-pleasing; so that a handful with all the incense is burnt, a sweet savour; of a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note he shall take thereout his handful of the flour thereof, and of the oil thereof, with all the frankincense thereof; and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar. The witnesses note to be an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the priest taking a handful of the flour and oil with all the frankincense and burning it for a memorial, a sweet savour — the part standing for the whole, but all the sweet incense given to God; the type that the sweet savour of Christ's graces and devotion ascended to God, a memorial accepted, well-pleasing.

So the witnesses make the verse the burning of a handful with all the incense, a sweet savour. The lesson the expositors draw is that a memorial-handful, with all the frankincense, was burnt to God, the sweet savour of Christ's graces ascending, accepted. The witnesses note the bring to the priests: "bring it to Aaron's sons" — Gill notes left with the priest; the offered. The expositors observe the his handful: "his handful of the flour" — the part for the whole; the a token. The witnesses press the all the frankincense: "all the frankincense" — wholly to God; the the whole. The expositors draw the lesson on the the memorial: "burn the memorial" — to put in remembrance; the a part. The expositors note the Christ's graces: the sweet savour of Christ's graces. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to be an offering made by fire of a sweet savour unto the LORD, and the memorial. The verse thus records the memorial-handful — teaching that a part with all the incense was burnt to God, the sweet savour of Christ's graces ascending.
3:
"And the remnant of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons': it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire." The witnesses note the remnant of the meat offering given to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy. Gill notes the remnant of the meat offering was Aaron's and his sons', which shows the care taken by the Lord for the maintenance of his priests, and it was a thing most holy. The witnesses note God giving the remnant of the meat offering, after the memorial was burnt, to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy; the priests' portion of the offering, God's provision for his ministers — that the rest is the priests', and it is most holy: "the remnant of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons': it is a thing most holy"; the witnesses note the portion, "the remnant... shall be Aaron's and his sons'" — that, after the handful was burnt to God, the rest was given to the priests for food, "the care taken by the Lord for the maintenance of his priests"; the witnesses note the holiness, "it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire" — that this portion was "most holy," to be eaten only by the priests, in the holy place; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that God provides for those who serve at his altar, "they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar" (1 Cor. 9:13), and that the holy things are for the holy; the witnesses note the type, that they who feed on Christ, the meat offering, must be the consecrated, the priests of God; the witnesses note that God's ministers are fed from the offerings; so that the remnant is the priests', a thing most holy; it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire. The witnesses note And the remnant of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons'. The witnesses note it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire.

The witnesses note the remnant of the meat offering, after the memorial was burnt, given to Aaron and his sons for food, a thing most holy — God's provision for the maintenance of his priests; they which wait at the altar partakers with the altar, the holy things for the holy, they who feed on Christ the meat offering being the consecrated priests of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the priests' portion, God's provision for his ministers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rest of the offering was the priests', a thing most holy, God providing for those who serve at his altar, the holy things for the holy. The witnesses note the the remnant: "the remnant... shall be Aaron's" — Gill notes the maintenance; the the remnant; the the maintenance; the of his priests; the for food; the the rest. The expositors observe the most holy: "a thing most holy" — eaten by the priests; the most holy; the eaten by the priests; the in the holy place; the the portion; the holy. The witnesses press the provides for his priests: God provides for his priests, the maintenance. The expositors draw the lesson on the wait at the altar: they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar. The expositors note the feed on Christ: they who feed on Christ the meat offering. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and the portion. The verse thus records the priests' portion — teaching that the rest was the priests', a thing most holy, God providing for those who serve at his altar.
4:
"And if thou bring an oblation of a meat offering baken in the oven, it shall be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil." The witnesses note the meat offering baked in the oven, of unleavened cakes or wafers of fine flour with oil. Gill notes this is another kind of meat offering, or in another form, baked in the oven, of unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil. The witnesses note God appointing the meat offering baked in the oven to be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil; the dressed meal-offering, baked, yet still of fine flour and oil and unleavened — that the offering may be baked, but still pure and oiled: "an oblation of a meat offering baken in the oven, it shall be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil"; the witnesses note the form, "baken in the oven" — that the offering might be brought, not as raw flour, but as cakes or wafers, dressed by fire; the witnesses note the cakes, "unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil" — the thicker cakes, with the oil mingled in; the witnesses note the wafers, "unleavened wafers anointed with oil" — the thinner wafers, with the oil spread on; the witnesses note the constant marks, that all was still of "fine flour," "unleavened," and with "oil," the same purity, freedom from leaven, and anointing of the Spirit; the witnesses note that, however dressed, the offering is pure and oiled; so that the baked offering is unleavened, of fine flour and oil; or unleavened wafers anointed with oil. The witnesses note And if thou bring an oblation of a meat offering baken in the oven, it shall be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil. The witnesses note or unleavened wafers anointed with oil.

The witnesses note the meat offering baked in the oven, of unleavened cakes (oil mingled in) or wafers (oil spread on), still of fine flour, unleavened, and oiled — the same purity, freedom from leaven, and anointing of the Spirit; the various forms shadowing the various sufferings of Christ, dressed by the fire of affliction, yet ever pure, without the leaven of sin, full of the Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse the dressed meal-offering, baked yet pure. The lesson the expositors draw is that, baked into cakes or wafers, the offering was still of fine flour, unleavened, and oiled, the various forms shadowing the various sufferings of Christ, ever pure and full of the Spirit. The witnesses note the baken in the oven: "baken in the oven" — Gill notes dressed by fire; the baken in the oven; the dressed by fire; the another form; the cakes; the wafers. The expositors observe the cakes mingled: "cakes... mingled with oil" — the oil in; the cakes mingled; the the oil in; the the thicker; the the cakes; the mingled. The witnesses press the wafers anointed: "wafers anointed with oil" — the oil on; the wafers anointed; the the oil on; the the thinner; the spread; the the wafers. The expositors draw the lesson on the still pure: still of fine flour, unleavened; the still pure; the fine flour; the unleavened; the with oil; the the same. The expositors note the ever pure: ever pure, without leaven, full of the Spirit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the or unleavened wafers anointed with oil, and the baked offering. The verse thus records the baked offering — teaching that, however dressed, it was pure and oiled, shadowing the sufferings of Christ, ever pure and full of the Spirit.
5:
"And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan, it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil." The witnesses note the meat offering baked on a pan, of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil. Gill notes the pan had no edge or covering, and the paste on it was hard, that it might be parted in pieces; the offering was of fine flour, unleavened, mingled with oil. The witnesses note God appointing the meat offering baked on a pan to be of fine flour, unleavened, mingled with oil; another form of the dressed offering, still pure and oiled — that the pan-baked offering too is of fine flour and oil: "if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan, it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil"; the witnesses note the form, "baken in a pan" — on a flat pan "without edge or covering," the paste baked hard; the witnesses note the constant marks, "of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil" — the same fine flour, the same freedom from leaven, the same oil mingled, as in the other forms; the witnesses note that, however the offering was dressed, the essentials were unchanged: purity (fine flour), no corruption (unleavened), and the Spirit (oil); the witnesses note the type before given, that the differing forms shadow the differing ways and trials in which Christ was offered, yet ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit; the witnesses note that God will have his offerings, in whatever form, pure and oiled; so that the pan-baked offering is of fine flour and oil; mingled with oil. The witnesses note And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan. The witnesses note it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil.

The witnesses note the meat offering baked on a flat pan, still of fine flour, unleavened, and mingled with oil — the essentials unchanged in every form: purity (fine flour), no corruption (unleavened), and the Spirit (oil); the differing forms shadowing the differing ways Christ was offered, yet ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse another form of the dressed offering, still pure and oiled. The lesson the expositors draw is that the pan-baked offering too was of fine flour, unleavened, and oiled, the essentials unchanged, a shadow of Christ ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit. The witnesses note the baken in a pan: "baken in a pan" — Gill notes a flat pan; the baken in a pan; the a flat pan; the no edge; the baked hard; the the paste. The expositors observe the fine flour: "of fine flour unleavened" — purity; the fine flour; the purity; the unleavened; the no corruption; the the same. The witnesses press the mingled with oil: "mingled with oil" — the Spirit; the mingled with oil; the the Spirit; the the oil; the in every form; the the same. The expositors draw the lesson on the essentials unchanged: the essentials unchanged; the essentials unchanged; the the essentials; the unchanged; the in every form; the the same. The witnesses observe the differing ways: the differing ways Christ offered; the differing ways; the differing; the the ways; the Christ; the offered. The expositors note the ever the same: ever the same, pure; the ever the same; the ever; the the same; the pure; the the Spirit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan, the it shall be of fine flour unleavened mingled with oil, and the pan offering. The verse thus records the pan-baked offering — teaching that it too was of fine flour and oil, the essentials unchanged, a shadow of Christ ever the same.
6:
"Thou shalt part it in pieces, and pour oil thereon: it is a meat offering." The witnesses note the offering parted in pieces and oil poured on it. Gill notes this answered to the dividing of the pieces of the burnt offering, and signified the same thing; the oil was poured on it. The witnesses note God appointing the baked offering to be parted in pieces and oil poured on it, for it is a meat offering; the breaking of the offering and the pouring on of oil — that the offering is divided and freshly oiled: "Thou shalt part it in pieces, and pour oil thereon: it is a meat offering"; the witnesses note the parting, "part it in pieces" — that the baked cake was broken into pieces, which "answered to the dividing of the pieces of the burnt offering, and signified the same thing"; the witnesses note the oil, "pour oil thereon" — that, even after baking, fresh oil was poured on, the offering never without the oil; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the breaking in pieces may shadow the breaking of Christ's body in his sufferings, and the abundant oil the fulness of the Spirit poured upon him without measure; the witnesses note that the offering, broken, was yet full of the oil, the Spirit not lacking; the witnesses note that what is offered to God is broken and yet anointed; so that the offering is parted and oil poured on; it is a meat offering. The witnesses note Thou shalt part it in pieces, and pour oil thereon. The witnesses note it is a meat offering.

The witnesses note the baked offering parted in pieces and fresh oil poured on it — the breaking answering to the dividing of the burnt offering; some seeing the breaking shadow the breaking of Christ's body in his sufferings, and the abundant oil the fulness of the Spirit poured upon him without measure, the offering broken yet never without the oil.

So the witnesses make the verse the breaking of the offering and the pouring on of oil. The lesson the expositors draw is that the offering was parted in pieces and freshly oiled, some seeing the breaking of Christ's body and the fulness of the Spirit, the offering broken yet full of the oil. The witnesses note the part it in pieces: "part it in pieces" — Gill notes as the burnt offering; the part it in pieces; the as the burnt offering; the broken; the the baked cake; the divided. The expositors observe the pour oil thereon: "pour oil thereon" — fresh oil; the pour oil thereon; the fresh oil; the after baking; the never without; the the oil. The witnesses press the breaking of Christ: the breaking of Christ's body; the breaking of Christ; the the breaking; the Christ's body; the his sufferings; the broken. The expositors draw the lesson on the fulness of the Spirit: the fulness of the Spirit; the fulness of the Spirit; the the fulness; the the Spirit; the without measure; the abundant. The witnesses observe the broken yet anointed: broken yet anointed; the broken yet anointed; the broken; the yet anointed; the full of oil; the not lacking. The expositors note the offered to God: what is offered to God; the offered to God; the offered; the to God; the broken; the anointed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Thou shalt part it in pieces and pour oil thereon, the it is a meat offering, and the breaking. The verse thus records the breaking and oiling — teaching that the offering was parted and freshly oiled, some seeing the breaking of Christ's body and the fulness of the Spirit.
7:
"And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in the fryingpan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil." The witnesses note the meat offering baked in the frying pan, of fine flour with oil. Gill notes the difference between the pan and the frying pan: the frying pan has a cover and is deep, the pan has no cover; the offering was made of fine flour with oil. The witnesses note God appointing the meat offering baked in the frying pan to be of fine flour with oil; yet another form of the dressed offering, still pure and oiled — that the frying-pan offering too is of fine flour and oil: "if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in the fryingpan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil"; the witnesses note the form, "baken in the fryingpan" — a deep, covered pan, distinct from the open pan (v.5); the witnesses note the constant marks, "of fine flour with oil" — the same fine flour and the same oil, in this as in the other forms; the witnesses note that, in every manner of dressing (raw, oven, pan, frying pan), the offering kept the same essentials: the fine flour of purity and the oil of the Spirit; the witnesses note the type before given, that the differing forms shadow the differing ways and trials in which Christ was offered, yet ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit; the witnesses note that God allows variety in the manner, but requires the same purity and anointing; so that the frying-pan offering is of fine flour and oil; it shall be made of fine flour with oil. The witnesses note And if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in the fryingpan. The witnesses note it shall be made of fine flour with oil.

The witnesses note the meat offering baked in the deep, covered frying pan, still of fine flour with oil — the same essentials in every manner of dressing: the fine flour of purity and the oil of the Spirit; the differing forms shadowing the differing ways Christ was offered, yet ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit, God allowing variety in the manner but requiring the same purity and anointing.

So the witnesses make the verse yet another form of the dressed offering, still pure and oiled. The lesson the expositors draw is that the frying-pan offering too was of fine flour with oil, the same essentials in every form, a shadow of Christ ever the same. The witnesses note the frying pan: "baken in the fryingpan" — Gill notes deep and covered; the frying pan; the deep and covered; the distinct from the pan; the the form; the another. The expositors observe the fine flour with oil: "of fine flour with oil" — the same; the fine flour with oil; the the same; the purity; the the oil; the the Spirit. The witnesses press the every manner: in every manner of dressing; the every manner; the every; the manner; the the same essentials; the kept. The expositors draw the lesson on the differing ways: the differing ways Christ offered; the differing ways; the differing; the the ways; the Christ; the offered. The expositors note the variety allowed: variety in the manner, the same purity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it shall be made of fine flour with oil, and the frying-pan offering. The verse thus records the frying-pan offering — teaching that it too was of fine flour and oil, the same essentials in every form.
8:
"And thou shalt bring the meat offering that is made of these things unto the LORD: and when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar." The witnesses note the meat offering brought to the LORD, presented to the priest, and brought to the altar. Keil and Delitzsch note the meat offering made of these things was to be brought unto the LORD, presented to the priest, who brought it to the altar. The witnesses note the offerer bringing the meat offering to the LORD, presenting it to the priest, who brought it to the altar; the presenting of the offering through the priest, the way of approach — that the offering is brought to God by the priest's hand: "thou shalt bring the meat offering... unto the LORD: and when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar"; the witnesses note the bringing, "bring the meat offering... unto the LORD" — to "the tabernacle, the house of the LORD," and to "the LORD's priest"; the witnesses note the presenting, "when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar" — that the offerer brought it, but the priest presented it at the altar; the witnesses note the order, that the offering came to God not directly but through the priest, who alone might bring it to the altar; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, that our offerings come to God by the hand of our great High Priest, who alone presents them and us to God, that they may be accepted; the witnesses note that we have access and acceptance only through the Mediator; so that the offering is brought to the altar by the priest; he shall bring it unto the altar. The witnesses note And thou shalt bring the meat offering that is made of these things unto the LORD. The witnesses note and when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar.

The witnesses note the meat offering brought to the LORD and presented to the priest, who brought it to the altar — the offering coming to God not directly but through the priest's hand; a type that our offerings come to God by the hand of our great High Priest, who alone presents them and us to God, we having access and acceptance only through the Mediator.

So the witnesses make the verse the presenting of the offering through the priest. The lesson the expositors draw is that the offering came to God through the priest's hand, a type of our offerings presented to God by our great High Priest, our only access. The witnesses note the bring to the LORD: "bring the meat offering... unto the LORD" — Keil notes to his house; the bring to the LORD; the to his house; the the tabernacle; the the priest; the brought. The expositors observe the presented to the priest: "presented unto the priest" — by the offerer; the presented to the priest; the by the offerer; the to him; the the offering; the brought. The witnesses press the priest brings it: "he shall bring it unto the altar" — the priest alone; the at the altar. The expositors draw the lesson on the by the High Priest: our offerings by the High Priest; the by the High Priest; the our offerings; the the High Priest; the presents; the to God. The expositors note the presents them and us: presents them and us to God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and when it is presented unto the priest he shall bring it unto the altar, and the presenting. The verse thus records the presenting through the priest — teaching that the offering came to God by the priest's hand, a type of our offerings presented by our great High Priest.
9:
"And the priest shall take from the meat offering a memorial thereof, and shall burn it upon the altar: it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the priest taking a memorial from the meat offering and burning it, a sweet savour. Gill notes the priest took a memorial, an handful of it, of the fine flour or of the baked pieces, whether baked in the oven, pan, or frying pan, and burnt it. The witnesses note the priest taking a memorial of the meat offering, in whatever form, and burning it on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD; the burning of the memorial-handful of the dressed offering too, well-pleasing — that a memorial is burnt to God, of every kind: "the priest shall take from the meat offering a memorial thereof, and shall burn it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the memorial, "a memorial thereof" — "an handful," whether "of the fine flour" or "of the baked pieces," in whatever form the offering was; the witnesses note the burning, "burn it upon the altar" — the part offered to God; the witnesses note the acceptance, "an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD" — well-pleasing and accepted; the witnesses note that the same memorial and the same sweet savour rose from the offering, however it was dressed; the witnesses note the type before given, that the sweet savour of Christ's graces and devotion ascended to God, a memorial accepted; the witnesses note that what is offered to God in faith is a sweet savour, in whatever form; so that a memorial of every kind is burnt, a sweet savour; of a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note And the priest shall take from the meat offering a memorial thereof, and shall burn it upon the altar. The witnesses note it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the priest taking a memorial-handful of the meat offering, in whatever form, and burning it on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the same memorial and the same sweet savour rising however the offering was dressed; the sweet savour of Christ's graces and devotion ascending to God, a memorial accepted, what is offered to God in faith a sweet savour in whatever form.

So the witnesses make the verse the burning of the memorial-handful of the dressed offering. The lesson the expositors draw is that a memorial was burnt to God, of every kind, a sweet savour, the sweet savour of Christ's graces ascending. The witnesses note the a memorial: "a memorial thereof" — Gill notes an handful; the a memorial; the an handful; the the fine flour; the the baked pieces; the in whatever form. The expositors observe the burn it: "burn it upon the altar" — the part to God; the burn it; the the part to God; the offered; the the altar; the the handful. The witnesses press the sweet savour: "of a sweet savour" — well-pleasing; the sweet savour; the well-pleasing; the accepted; the the offering; the ascended. The expositors draw the lesson on the same savour: the same savour, however dressed; the same savour; the the same; the savour; the however dressed; the rose. The expositors note the in faith: offered in faith, a sweet savour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is an offering made by fire of a sweet savour unto the LORD, and the memorial. The verse thus records the memorial burnt — teaching that a memorial of every kind was burnt to God, a sweet savour, the sweet savour of Christ's graces ascending.
10:
"And that which is left of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons': it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire." The witnesses note that which was left of the meat offering given to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy. Gill notes that which is left of the meat offering, not burnt with fire, was Aaron's and his sons'. The witnesses note God giving the remnant of the dressed meat offering, after the memorial was burnt, to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy; the priests' portion of the dressed offering too, God's provision repeated — that the rest is the priests', and it is most holy: "that which is left of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons': it is a thing most holy"; the witnesses note the portion, "that which is left... not burnt with fire" — the part not offered to God, given to the priests; the witnesses note the holiness, "a thing most holy" — that this, like the remnant of the raw offering (v.3), was most holy, the priests' food in the holy place; the witnesses note that the same provision was made of every form of the offering, the memorial to God and the rest to his priests; the witnesses note the meaning before given, that God provides for those who serve at his altar, and that they who feed on Christ, the meat offering, must be the consecrated; the witnesses note that God's ministers are fed from the holy things; so that the remnant of the dressed offering too is the priests'; it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire. The witnesses note And that which is left of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons'. The witnesses note it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire.

The witnesses note the remnant of the dressed meat offering, after the memorial was burnt, given to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy — God's provision repeated for every form of the offering, the memorial to God and the rest to his priests; God's ministers fed from the holy things, they who feed on Christ the meat offering being the consecrated.

So the witnesses make the verse the priests' portion of the dressed offering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rest of every form of the offering was the priests', a thing most holy, God providing for those who serve at his altar. The witnesses note the that which is left: "that which is left" — Gill notes not burnt; the that which is left; the not burnt; the the part; the to the priests; the the remnant. The expositors observe the most holy: "a thing most holy" — the priests' food; the most holy; the the priests' food; the the holy place; the the portion; the holy. The witnesses press the same provision: the same provision repeated; the same provision; the the same; the provision; the every form; the repeated. The expositors draw the lesson on the serve at his altar: those who serve at his altar; the serve at his altar; the those who serve; the the altar; the provided; the fed. The expositors note the fed from holy things: God's ministers fed from the holy things. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and the portion. The verse thus records the priests' portion — teaching that the rest of every form was the priests', a thing most holy, God providing for those who serve at his altar.
11:
"No meat offering, which ye shall bring unto the LORD, shall be made with leaven: for ye shall burn no leaven, nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire." The witnesses note that no meat offering should be made with leaven, nor any leaven or honey burnt on the altar. Gill notes leaven might be used in peace offerings and in the wave loaves, but not in meat offerings, neither in the handful that was burnt nor in the rest; nor any honey burnt on the altar. The witnesses note God forbidding leaven in the meat offering and the burning of any leaven or honey in the offerings made by fire; the excluding of leaven and honey, the type of sin and of mere natural sweetness — that nothing fermenting or corrupting may go to the altar: "No meat offering... shall be made with leaven: for ye shall burn no leaven, nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire"; the witnesses note the leaven forbidden, "no meat offering... shall be made with leaven" — that leaven, which ferments and sours, was kept out, "neither in the handful... nor in the rest"; the witnesses note the honey forbidden, "nor any honey... burn[t]" — that honey, which ferments and turns sour, was also kept from the altar; the witnesses note that the offering of God, and Christ the offering, must be free from the leaven of sin and the honey of carnal sweetness; the witnesses note that our service too must be sincere and uncorrupt; so that no leaven or honey is burnt on the altar; nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire. The witnesses note No meat offering, which ye shall bring unto the LORD, shall be made with leaven. The witnesses note for ye shall burn no leaven, nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire.

The witnesses note God forbidding leaven and honey in the offerings made by fire — leaven the type of sin and corruption ("the leaven of malice and wickedness"), honey of mere natural and fleshly sweetness, which ferments and puffs; both kept from the offering of Christ, who was without sin, and from our service, which must be sincere and uncorrupt.

So the witnesses make the verse the excluding of leaven and honey. The lesson the expositors draw is that nothing fermenting or corrupting might go to the altar, leaven the type of sin and honey of carnal sweetness, both kept from the offering of Christ and from our service. The witnesses note the no leaven: "shall be made with leaven" — Gill notes kept out; the no leaven; the kept out; the the handful; the the rest; the ferments. The expositors observe the no honey: "nor any honey" — kept from the altar; the no honey; the kept from the altar; the ferments; the turns sour; the burnt. The witnesses press the leaven the type of sin: leaven the type of sin; the leaven the type of sin; the leaven; the the type; the of sin; the corruption. The expositors draw the lesson on the honey carnal sweetness: honey, carnal sweetness; the honey carnal sweetness; the honey; the carnal; the sweetness; the puffs. The expositors note the sincere service: our service sincere and uncorrupt. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for ye shall burn no leaven nor any honey in any offering of the LORD made by fire, and the excluding. The verse thus records the excluding of leaven and honey — teaching that nothing corrupting might go to the altar, both kept from the offering of Christ and from our service.
12:
"As for the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them unto the LORD: but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour." The witnesses note the oblation of firstfruits offered to the LORD, but not burnt on the altar for a sweet savour. Gill notes that along with the oblation of the firstfruits, leaven and honey might be offered, but these were not to be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour. The witnesses note God appointing the oblation of firstfruits (with leaven and honey) to be offered to the LORD, but not burnt on the altar for a sweet savour; the distinction between what was given to God and what was burnt on the altar — that leaven and honey might be brought, but not laid on the fire: "the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them unto the LORD: but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour"; the witnesses note the offering, "the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them" — that the firstfruits, even with leaven and honey, might be brought and given to God (and were the priests' portion); the witnesses note the limit, "they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour" — that, though offered, they were not to be laid on the altar-fire, the leaven and honey being kept from the burning; the witnesses note that the things of nature may be sanctified and used, but only what is pure and uncorrupt rises as a sweet savour to God; so that the firstfruits are offered but not burnt for a sweet savour; but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour. The witnesses note As for the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them unto the LORD. The witnesses note but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour.

The witnesses note the firstfruits (with leaven and honey) offered to the LORD but not burnt on the altar for a sweet savour — the distinction between what God's people may give and enjoy and what is fit to be a sweet savour on the altar; the things of nature may be sanctified and used, but only what is pure and uncorrupt rises as a sweet savour to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the distinction between what was given and what was burnt. The lesson the expositors draw is that leaven and honey might be brought with the firstfruits but not laid on the fire, the things of nature given but not burnt, only the pure rising as a sweet savour. The witnesses note the offer them: "ye shall offer them" — Gill notes the firstfruits; the offer them; the the firstfruits; the with leaven; the and honey; the brought. The expositors observe the not burnt: "shall not be burnt on the altar" — kept from the fire; the not burnt; the kept from the fire; the the leaven; the the honey; the the burning. The witnesses press the the distinction: the distinction given and burnt; the the distinction; the given; the and burnt; the what is fit; the the altar. The expositors draw the lesson on the nature sanctified: the things of nature sanctified; the nature sanctified; the the things of nature; the sanctified; the used; the given. The expositors note the rises to God: only the pure rises as a sweet savour to God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour, and the distinction. The verse thus records the distinction — teaching that leaven and honey might be given but not burnt, only the pure rising as a sweet savour to God.
13:
"And every oblation of thy meat offering shalt thou season with salt; neither shalt thou suffer the salt of the covenant of thy God to be lacking from thy meat offering: with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt." The witnesses note every meat offering seasoned with salt, the salt of the covenant, never to be lacking. Gill notes salt makes food savoury and preserves from putrefaction, denoting the savouriness and acceptableness of Christ as a meat offering to his people, and the salt of the covenant was never to be lacking. The witnesses note God commanding every meat offering to be seasoned with salt, the salt of the covenant of God, never lacking, with all offerings salt to be offered; the salt of the covenant, the savour and incorruption of grace, the perpetuity of the covenant — that all the offerings are seasoned with salt: "every oblation of thy meat offering shalt thou season with salt; neither shalt thou suffer the salt of the covenant of thy God to be lacking... with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt"; the witnesses note the command, "season with salt" — that "salt makes food savoury, and preserves from putrefaction," and so was put on every offering; the witnesses note the meaning, "denoting the savouriness and acceptableness of Christ as a meat offering to his people," and the grace that seasons and preserves; the witnesses note the great phrase, "the salt of the covenant of thy God" — that salt, which preserves, was the token of the perpetual, incorruptible covenant ("a covenant of salt for ever," Num. 18:19; 2 Chron. 13:5); the witnesses note the universality, "with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt" — that no offering was to lack it; the witnesses note that what we offer God must be savoury with grace and free from corruption; so that all the offerings are seasoned with the salt of the covenant; with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt. The witnesses note every oblation of thy meat offering shalt thou season with salt; neither shalt thou suffer the salt of the covenant of thy God to be lacking from thy meat offering. The witnesses note with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt.

The witnesses note every meat offering seasoned with salt, "the salt of the covenant," never to be lacking — salt the savour and incorruption of grace (denoting Christ savoury to his people, and the grace that seasons and preserves), and the token of the perpetual, incorruptible covenant ("a covenant of salt for ever"); grace must season all our service, preserving it from the corruption of sin and hypocrisy.

So the witnesses make the verse the salt of the covenant, the savour and incorruption of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that all the offerings were seasoned with salt, the token of the perpetual covenant and of the grace that must season all our service, free from corruption. The witnesses note the season with salt: "season with salt" — Gill notes savoury and preserves; the season with salt; the savoury; the preserves; the from putrefaction; the every offering. The expositors observe the Christ savoury: Christ savoury to his people. The witnesses press the salt of the covenant: "the salt of the covenant" — the perpetual covenant; the the token. The expositors draw the lesson on the all offerings: "with all thine offerings... salt" — none to lack; the all offerings; the none to lack; the salt; the every; the offered. The expositors note the from corruption: free from the corruption of sin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt, and the salt. The verse thus records the salt of the covenant — teaching that all the offerings were seasoned with salt, the token of the perpetual covenant and of the grace that must season all our service.
